message.content: Adversaries may create or modify system-level processes to ensure the repeated execution of malicious payloads, thereby achieving persistence. This technique involves the installation or alteration of services, daemons, or agents that are configured to run at startup or at regular intervals. On Windows and Linux, these are known as services, while on macOS, they are referred to as Launch Daemons and Launch Agents. These processes can be created with administrator privileges but executed with root/SYSTEM privileges, allowing adversaries to escalate their privileges. Common exploitation methods include installing new services or modifying existing ones to run malicious code. For instance, Exaramel for Linux uses a hardcoded location to maintain persistence if the system uses Upstart or System V and runs as root. Mitigation strategies include monitoring and restricting the creation and modification of system processes, employing application whitelisting, and ensuring that only trusted administrators have the necessary privileges to alter system processes. Regular audits and integrity checks of system processes can also help detect unauthorized changes.
